had a creative one, but they were kind of so-so.

The content marketing world has changed so much.
It's a bit of a wild west where
Your clicks to the website are disappearing and your ICP is having that first sales conversation with ChatGPT or something similar.
So I did like a study about how the AI search visits are 4.4 times more likely to convert.
So showing up in the answers has a ton of value and they also spend three times as long.
And so it's almost like, right, if you're not in these answers, prospects are giving them so much credibility that it feels like you don't exist.
And we have found a way to very systematically, you know, get you in the answer through our process.
And ultimately, we're driving organic growth for all of our clients.
And so that's our big focus as a business.
This is one of our customers monograph just shows that like our approach is also thinking about keywords and SEO strategies.
It's not just a matter of showing up in AI answers, but we have had results on both sides and we do believe.

They benefit each other at the end of the day, if you go about it in more of an audience-first manner, where you think about being the expert around all the topics that your audience might care about.

And so part of our approach, the systematic side of it, is making sure that when we create content, our AI workflows that we're using and our experts in the loop are really focusing on the most important factors with GEO.
So we've got a deep researcher, and I'll give you a look at how that is involved in content creation.
We do a lot of fact-checking because AI is notorious for hallucinating or not being accurate, which is something a lot of people I talk to bring up as a concern.
We'll do a lot of content refreshes.
So you can really think about, like, after the six-month mark, you can start updating your content on a consistent basis, and you can continue to get value from the content in that way.
And, you know, the

The approach with growth when it comes to the new world is, we talked about the first point, it's more about being in the answer, in terms of the second, third, and the fourth point, nobody's hiring big teams because they want to move fast.
You can buy tools like AirOps, but those tools can actually be a lot more expensive than using us as a partner.
And they can also be very hard to assemble and update and, you know, models change, right?
You have to drag and drop instead of having something that's built in code, like the way that we run our process.
And then with agencies, you know, you're not looking at an agency that's just focused on SEO strategy or just focused on content creation.
With us, we're an end-to-end partner from strategy to staging and publishing in your CMS and even providing LLM visibility and analytics.
So that's a little background and high level on GrowthX.
Any questions that you want to ask me at this point?

Marcel, do you know much about, like, his background before starting GrowthX?
No, not too much.
Yeah.
So he's always believed that, like, brands should operate more like publishers.
When he was at IBM, he was like, wow, we could actually go out and buy publications for, like, the same amount that we spend on paid.
And so this has always been his philosophy.
And at IBM, he created securityintelligence.com.
Which was like their highest ROI marketing channel, won best B2B website award.
They'd recently deprecated it after 10 years, but it brought in like 17 million in their first year in revenue.
And so he took that approach at IBM and every place he went after, he built an organic content engine.
At HP, he built a newsletter called AI Minds and had over a thousand experts contribute to it.
At HashiCorp, he was the marketing leader from 6 million to over 100 million in ARR.
Onwards, he was the first and last CMO, as he likes to describe it, at scale, leading into the acquisition by Meta.

And then at DeepGram, what he did is, because it can be very time consuming and very expensive to build out the team that's necessary and the infrastructure to create, you know, super high quality editorial content.
He distilled all of these different steps into AI workflows and he spent his focus at DeepGram being able to execute.
A lot of the organic content initiatives using AI had great success, as you can see at Deepgram, started doing these workshops.
People were so interested in what he was doing that they were willing to pay him to do it for them, and that was the inception of GrowthX.
Fast forward to today, and we are over 10 million in ARR after a year, and we are a team of over 80 that has a platform that powers a lot of work that we do, and then experts in the loop.
So every client is going to get a managing director, managing editor, and a content manager.
These are some places, right, like where our team has worked, and just to give you like a couple quick examples, our chief content officer was the editor-in-chief at TechCrunch for 10 years.
And then we've got folks who have like built the g2.com website or ran SEO at Fanatics and TripAdvisor.
And so that's a little bit about what makes us different is that.
We have our own platform that powers our service, but we're using AI and experts in the loop so that we can essentially be like services as software.
The platform is something that we're like opening up to clients.

We use it today for our execution, but it's basically going to house all the collaboration that we do today and things like Slack and Airtable and Notion, in addition to the reporting and the analytics that we do around SEO and LLM visibility so that you've got like a one-stop shop for everything that's happening with GrowthX, including like the assignments and the status of the assignments that we're working on.
Does that make sense so far?
Yeah, it totally makes sense.
I'm really curious.

Or is it like primarily content?
Yeah, great question.
So we have two things.
phases to our engagements.

The first phase would be the strategy sprint where our main focus is generating artifacts that provide the workflows with the rich context about your personas, your company, your writing guidelines, and brand voice, and also aligning on the topics that we want to create content around and prioritizing those opportunities against keyword volume, difficulty.
So the SEO audit would be a part of phase one.
And then phase two is all about getting to daily publishing for you.
We'll be ready to start that as soon as we get into phase two.
A lot of the work that we focus on to start is editorial, right?
So programmatic SEO is something we've done for our clients.
Newsletters are things we've done for our clients.
But the standard engagement would be the editorial as the main lane unless we wanted to add something to.
You know, that phase two starting package.

Interesting.

then you kind of like find the next gear forward.

comes to mind right now for me, I'm like, how do we get, you know, either citations or just other backlinks that kind of help build our like organic footprint outside of just our site, right?

I think the answer to your question, because a lot of what we do, just to be totally honest, is focused on content creation or content refresh.
It's really our process, you know, like the process, we go super deep on the research.
We are going to go super deep on the competition.

Humans at the scale that we can get to it while maintaining quality.

And then when we actually start creating content, you know, this is much different than going into ChatGPT, asking it to write you an article, or even like using, you know, a workflow orchestration tool.

Some workflows for the clients that we're serving.

And so if you look at this assignment brief step, that's just taking keywords, direction, and then those artifacts, what's great about our process here is that to create this robust brief that you're seeing here, we are fetching keyword traffic, we're analyzing the organic results, the SERP ranking factors, the competitor content, and then all of that is informing the brief that's generated in the first step.

When you look at the research step, we were using that brief to go in and generate research questions and then perform deep research on each of those questions while validating it against objective third-party sources.
When you look at the article being drafted, our editors are able to work directly in the context of the draft to make changes or even to...
You know, use the context to make suggestions here and have the content update.
And then when you think about the fact checking and the fact that like we're generating images for our clients, we take so much off of your plate and we do it at such a high quality.
